_schema.key: deck identifier, used in filenames and app state
_schema.label: display name
_schema.enabled: whether this deck is selectable in the UI
_schema.user_notes: array of {title, body} shown in the ℹ️ panel

user_notes.title: Reading the pronunciation guide
user_notes.body: The italicised line beneath each Greek word is a phonetic guide written with familiar letters. The accent mark (´) shows which syllable to stress — say it slightly louder and longer than the others.

user_notes.title: Two sounds that may be unfamiliar
user_notes.body: 'ch' (e.g. Chaetós) sounds like the 'ch' in Scottish 'loch' — a soft rasp at the back of the throat, not as in 'church'. 'th' is always the gentle sound in 'thin', never the harder sound in 'the'.

user_notes.title: Two ways to say nine
user_notes.body: Greeks say 'enniá' for nine on its own, but 'ennéa' in compound numbers such as nineteen (dekaennéa). Both forms are correct — the everyday spoken form is used on the standalone nine card.

user_notes.title: About the alphabet cards
user_notes.body: The Alphabet section shows each Greek letter in its upper and lowercase forms. Some look like English letters but sound different — Ρ ρ is 'r', Ν ν is 'n', and Χ χ makes the raspy 'ch' sound described above.

user_notes.title: Reading the pronunciation guide
user_notes.body: The italicised line beneath each Albanian word is a phonetic guide written with familiar letters. The accent mark (´) shows which syllable to stress. Albanian already uses the Latin alphabet, so the guide focuses on sounds that differ from English.

user_notes.title: Key sounds to know
user_notes.body: 'ë' sounds like 'uh' (the vowel in 'the'). 'q' sounds like 'ky' (as in 'backyard' said quickly). 'xh' sounds like 'j' in 'jungle'. 'dh' sounds like 'th' in 'the'. 'th' sounds like 'th' in 'thin'. 'y' sounds like the German ü or French u — shown as 'ü' in the guide. 'rr' is a rolled r, stronger than 'r'.

user_notes.title: About the alphabet cards
user_notes.body: The Albanian alphabet has 36 letters, including digraphs treated as single letters: Dh, Gj, Ll, Nj, Rr, Sh, Th, Xh, and Zh. Each counts as one letter — so the alphabet is longer than English but all letters look familiar.

user_notes.title: Aunt and uncle
user_notes.body: Albanian distinguishes paternal and maternal relatives. 'Hallë' is the father's sister; 'tezë' is the mother's sister. 'Xhaxha' is the father's brother; 'dajë' is the mother's brother. The cards use the paternal forms — adjust to whichever fits your family.

user_notes.title: Reading the pronunciation guide
user_notes.body: The italicised line beneath each Spanish word is a phonetic guide written with familiar letters. The accent mark (´) shows which syllable to stress — say it slightly louder and longer than the others. Spanish vowels are pure and steady: a = 'ah', e = 'eh', i = 'ee', o = 'oh', u = 'oo'.

user_notes.title: Key sounds to know
user_notes.body: 'j' (and 'g' before e or i) sounds like a strong English 'h' — as in 'Jirafa' (Hi-rá-fa). 'll' and 'y' both sound like the 'y' in 'yes'. 'ñ' sounds like 'ny' in 'canyon'. 'rr' is a rolled r — shown as 'rr' in the guide. 'v' and 'b' make the same sound in Spanish. 'h' is always silent. 'qu' sounds like 'k'.

user_notes.title: Latin American Spanish
user_notes.body: This deck uses Latin American Spanish forms for the handful of words that differ across dialects: 'papa' (potato), 'carro' (car), 'jugo' (juice), 'durazno' (peach), and 'nevera' (fridge). In Spain these are 'patata', 'coche', 'zumo', 'melocotón', and 'nevera' (same). All other words are the same across dialects.

user_notes.title: About the alphabet cards
user_notes.body: The Spanish alphabet has 27 letters — the same as English plus Ñ. The pronunciation guide shows how each letter's name is said in Spanish. A few letter names may surprise you: 'j' is called 'hota', 'y' is 'i griega' (Greek i), and 'w' is 'doble u'.
